engineering.translation: The verse inverts 3.6. yas tu — but the one who, having controlled the senses with the mind (manasā niyamya), engages the action-organs in karma-yoga (karmendriyaiḥ karma-yogam) without attachment — that one is viśiṣyate, the distinguished, the superior. The order matters: mind first, then action. Per the Advaita commentary, this is the genuine path, free from the hypocrite's defects of 3.6. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading, this is the seeker who has subdued the inner instrument and can therefore perform karma-yoga genuinely. The verse echoes 2.61's manaḥ saṁyamya — but here the inner control issues into engaged production rather than into withdrawal. The engineering reading: the discipline is real attention-control first, then engagement of the action-organs in unattached production. Surface restraint with interior chaos (3.6) is rejected; interior restraint that liberates the action-organs to ship cleanly is endorsed.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A staff engineer arrives at her desk at precisely 9 AM. Before opening any application, she sits for two minutes with the laptop closed and observes carefully what her attention is presently doing. She registers, unhurriedly: a low-grade anxiety regarding the design review scheduled at 2 PM; residual frustration concerning a code-review comment received yesterday from a junior colleague; a faint inclination toward checking the news. She does not respond to any of these movements. She simply observes. Then, at 9:02, she opens the terminal and runs the Postgres migration she had committed to executing. She writes the four particular lines of code that the migration requires. She watches the continuous-integration pipeline complete. She files the GitHub pull request for review. She does not check Slack messages until the migration's completion is confirmed. The 9 AM two-minute pre-step constituted the manasā niyamya: the mind controlled. The 9:02 onward constituted the action-organs engaged in karma-yoga, performed without attachment. She did not have to suppress the anxiety, the frustration, the inclination. She simply declined to permit them to determine the action's direction. The verse 3.7 designates such a person viśiṣyate — distinguished. This capacity is a real, learnable discipline.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses this verse to justify long meditation sessions before any work, or to inflate the 'attention discipline' part into a full-time practice that displaces shipping. The verse is precise: mind-control PRECEDES action and ENABLES action — not displaces it. An engineer who spent two hours preparing their attention and shipped nothing has missed the verse's structure. The two-minute audit is the form; the action is the content.
engineering.counter_example: When the engineer is in genuine flow and the attention is already settled, the pre-step is not required. The verse's structure assumes the attention needs settling; if it does not, sitting still for two minutes adds nothing. The verse describes the relation between mind and action; it does not prescribe a fixed ritual. Karma-yoga issues from a settled mind, however that settling happened.
engineering.implication: Before opening Slack, sit with the laptop closed for two minutes and notice what your attention is doing. Do not suppress it; just register it. Then engage the action-organs cleanly. The 3.7 discipline is the inversion of 3.6's pathology.
engineering.quotable_line: Mind first, then action; the two-minute audit at 9 AM is the inversion of the 3.6 hypocrite at 6 PM.

iterations.mutation: v1: lengthened sentences with appositional clauses; added polysyllabic words (continuous-integration, unhurriedly, designate, capacity); added GitHub + Postgres + CI explicitly. FK lifted to 9+.
